attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- foundation-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level foundation document for a company, product, or new primitive. This document captures thesis, mission, boundaries, actor model, surfaces, strategic bets, and open questions without collapsing ambiguity into implementation decisions. Applies when the user is still defining what the system is, what it is not, and what long-term direction it implies. Does NOT apply to concrete service specifications in `SPEC.md`, implementation plans, PRDs, or roadmap execution docs.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- spec-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level SPEC.md service specification — the language-agnostic behavioral spec that sits at a project's root and describes what a long-running service or automation does (problem statement, goals/non-goals, components, domain model). Applies when the user says things like "write a spec for X", "draft a specification", "update SPEC.md to add Y", "revise the non-goals", "add a new component to the spec", or hands over notes and asks for them to be turned into a formal service spec. Also applies when the user references an existing SPEC.md and wants edits, even if they don't explicitly use the word "spec". Covers both greenfield creation and targeted updates of an existing file. Does NOT apply to per-topic specs under thoughts/shared/specs/ (those are produced by create_ralph_topics from a JTBD), nor to JTBDs, implementation plans, or PRDs.
- lightfast-aeo: Use when authoring or reviewing any Lightfast content destined for AI engine retrieval —
release notes, blog posts, docs pages, integration pages, anything published to the
public site. Triggers on requests to write, draft, rewrite, or audit prose that should
be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews, or other answer engines,
even when the user doesn't say "AEO", "citations", or "answer engine". Per-content-type
skills compose this for voice and section ordering — load this one too whenever they
activate.
- lightfast-changelog: Write and review AEO-optimized changelog entries for Lightfast. Triggers when the user asks
to write a changelog entry, review changelog content, or asks about changelog structure,
AEO optimization, or release notes formatting.
- lightfast-sdk: Use Lightfast tools to search across team knowledge and interact with connected providers
(GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo). Triggers when the user asks about errors, issues,
PRs, deployments, or wants to search across their dev tools. Also triggers proactively when
investigating bugs, reviewing code, or checking deployment status.
- lightfast-debug: General-purpose Lightfast debugging workflow. Triggers when the user asks to debug,
investigate, trace, reproduce, inspect, or diagnose behavior across the app, platform,
Inngest workflows, database state, connected providers, or observability tooling. Use as
the broad entrypoint when no narrower skill is clearly better, when the failing surface is
unclear, or when a bug may cross multiple systems. Prefer `lightfast-db`,
`lightfast-inngest`, or `lightfast-sdk` for direct single-surface requests that clearly map
to one of those tools.
- lightfast-inngest: Inspect Inngest function runs, debug failures, send events, and invoke functions via the
local Inngest dev server MCP. Triggers when the user asks about background jobs, workflow
runs, failed steps, event delivery, or wants to invoke/test Inngest functions.
- lightfast-clerk: Provision Clerk test users, sign in via the browser, mint JWTs, and tear
everything down — for local-dev tRPC / desktop / API testing. Triggers when
the user wants to call Lightfast tRPC procedures with a real auth token,
test desktop sign-in flows, or set up / clean up test users in Clerk dev.
Dev-only: refuses to run against pk_live_ keys or non-localhost URLs.
- lightfast-db: Query and inspect the Postgres database schema and data. Triggers when the user asks about
tables, columns, rows, database schema, wants to run a SELECT query, or needs to understand
the data model. Read-only — cannot modify data.
- turborepo: Turborepo monorepo build system guidance. Triggers on: turbo.json, task pipelines,
dependsOn, caching, remote cache, the "turbo" CLI, --filter, --affected, CI optimization, environment
variables, internal packages, monorepo structure/best practices, and boundaries.

Use when user: configures tasks/workflows/pipelines, creates packages, sets up
monorepo, shares code between apps, runs changed/affected packages, debugs cache,
or has apps/packages directories.
- implement_plan: Implement technical plans from thoughts/shared/plans with verification
- create_plan: Create detailed implementation plans through interactive research and iteration
- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues
- commit: Create git commits with user approval and no Claude attribution
- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot
- improve_plan: Adversarial review of implementation plans — find weaknesses, kill code smells, improve DX
- research_codebase: Document codebase as-is with thoughts directory for historical context
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Check repository rulesets applied to main

content.text:
Found it. PR 617 is blocked by repository ruleset #15254385 on `main`, which requires:

**1. 1 approving review** — `reviewDecision` is empty. CodeRabbit submitted a `COMMENTED` review (not `APPROVED`), and no human has approved yet.

**2. All review threads resolved** — CodeRabbit posted 1 actionable comment that may still be an open thread.

All CI checks (CI, Core CI, CodeQL, Vercel previews, CodeRabbit) are ✅ green, and `mergeable: MERGEABLE`. The block is purely review/approval, not checks.

Let me verify the unresolved-thread angle:

input.command: gh api graphql -f query='query { repository(owner:"lightfastai", name:"lightfast") { pullRequest(number:617) { reviewThreads(first:50) { nodes { isResolved isOutdated path comments(first:1){ nodes{ author{login} body }} } } } } }' | python3 -m json.tool

{
    "data": {
        "repository": {
            "pullRequest": {
                "reviewThreads": {
                    "nodes": [
                        {
                            "isResolved": false,
                            "isOutdated": false,
                            "path": "apps/desktop/README.md",
                            "comments": {
                                "nodes": [
                                    {
                                        "author": {
                                            "login": "coderabbitai"
                                        },
                                        "body": "_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🔴 Critical_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Verify what `dev:full` runs and whether proxy configuration is present.
set -euo pipefail

echo "== root package.json dev scripts =="
rg -n '"dev:full"|"dev:desktop-stack"|"proxy"' package.json -C 2 || true

echo
echo "== turbo config candidates =="
fd -HI 'turbo.json|turbo.*.json|turbo.config.*' . | sed 's#^\./##'

echo
echo "== microfrontends config references =="
rg -n "microfrontends|fallback|3024|lightfast-app|lightfast-www" apps/app/microfrontends.json -C 2

echo
echo "== workspace scripts mentioning proxy task directly =="
rg -n '"proxy"|microfrontends|vercel.*microfrontends' --glob '**/package.json' -C 1
```

Repository: lightfastai/lightfast

Length of output: 3079

---

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Read the actual README to see what lines 69-77 claim
echo "== README.md lines 65-80 =="
sed -n '65,80p' apps/desktop/README.md

echo
echo "== Search for port 3024 in entire repo =="
rg '3024' --type md --type json --type js

echo
echo "== Search for 'proxy' task in turbo configs =="
rg '"proxy"' turbo.json apps/*/turbo.json || true

echo
echo "== Check root turbo.json for pipeline definitions =="
jq '.pipeline | keys' turbo.json 2>/dev/null || cat turbo.json | head -50
```

Repository: lightfastai/lightfast

Length of output: 20383

---

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Check if dev:desktop-stack exists and what the root package.json actually has
echo "== root package.json dev scripts (lines 15-30) =="
sed -n '15,30p' package.json

echo
echo "== apps/app/package.json dev-related scripts =="
rg -n '"proxy"|"dev"' apps/app/package.json | head -20

echo
echo "== Check if dev:desktop-stack is mentioned anywhere =="
rg -n 'dev:desktop-stack' package.json apps/*/package.json || echo "(not found)"
```

Repository: lightfastai/lightfast

Length of output: 1221

---

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Check the full dev-related scripts in apps/app/package.json
echo "== apps/app/package.json dev scripts section =="
jq '.scripts | with_entries(select(.key | contains("dev") or contains("proxy")))' apps/app/package.json

echo
echo "== Check entire scripts section of apps/app/package.json =="
jq '.scripts | keys' apps/app/package.json | head -20
```

Repository: lightfastai/lightfast

Length of output: 540

---



</details>

**[blocker] README falsely claims Turbo auto-injects the proxy at 3024.** 
Lines 75-77 assert `pnpm dev:full` boots the microfrontends proxy via "Turbo 2.9's built-in microfrontends integration," but this feature is not implemented. The proxy script does not exist in `apps/app/package.json`, no `dev:desktop-stack` script exists in the root, and no proxy task is defined in any `turbo.json`. Running `pnpm dev:full` will only start the three app servers (4107/4101/4112) — port 3024 will not be live. Desktop auth will fail because `apps/desktop/.env.development` defaults to `http://localhost:3024` and the tRPC CORS whitelist only allows that origin (not 4107).

<details>
<summary>Recommended fix</summary>

```diff
-`pnpm dev:full` boots all three Next.js apps (4107/4101/4112) and the
-microfrontends proxy at 3024 — Turbo 2.9's built-in microfrontends
-integration auto-injects the proxy task alongside `@lightfast/app#dev`.
+`pnpm dev:full` boots all three Next.js apps (4107/4101/4112).
+To run the microfrontends proxy at 3024, add a root script that runs
+`pnpm --filter `@lightfast/app` proxy` (once the proxy task is defined in
+`apps/app/package.json`). Until then, `pnpm dev:full` alone is insufficient
+for desktop development.
```
</details>

}

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Verify what `dev:full` runs and whether proxy configuration is present.
set -euo pipefail

echo "== root package.json dev scripts =="
rg -n '"dev:full"|"dev:desktop-stack"|"proxy"' package.json -C 2 || true

echo
echo "== turbo config candidates =="
fd -HI 'turbo.json|turbo.*.json|turbo.config.*' . | sed 's#^\./##'

echo
echo "== microfrontends config references =="
rg -n "microfrontends|fallback|3024|lightfast-app|lightfast-www" apps/app/microfrontends.json -C 2

echo
echo "== workspace scripts mentioning proxy task directly =="
rg -n '"proxy"|microfrontends|vercel.*microfrontends' --glob '**/package.json' -C 1
```

**[blocker] README falsely claims Turbo auto-injects the proxy at 3024.** 
Lines 75-77 assert `pnpm dev:full` boots the microfrontends proxy via "Turbo 2.9's built-in microfrontends integration," but this feature is not implemented. The proxy script does not exist in `apps/app/package.json`, no `dev:desktop-stack` script exists in the root, and no proxy task is defined in any `turbo.json`. Running `pnpm dev:full` will only start the three app servers (4107/4101/4112) — port 3024 will not be live. Desktop auth will fail because `apps/desktop/.env.development` defaults to `http://localhost:3024` and the tRPC CORS whitelist only allows that origin (not 4107).

<details>
<summary>Recommended fix</summary>

```diff
-`pnpm dev:full` boots all three Next.js apps (4107/4101/4112) and the
-microfrontends proxy at 3024 — Turbo 2.9's built-in microfrontends
-integration auto-injects the proxy task alongside `@lightfast/app#dev`.
+`pnpm dev:full` boots all three Next.js apps (4107/4101/4112).
+To run the microfrontends proxy at 3024, add a root script that runs
+`pnpm --filter `@lightfast/app` proxy` (once the proxy task is defined in
+`apps/app/package.json`). Until then, `pnpm dev:full` alone is insufficient
+for desktop development.
```
</details>

        }
    }
}

content.text:
Confirmed. Two blockers, both from the ruleset — not CI:

**1. Needs 1 approving review.** Current `reviewDecision` is empty. CodeRabbit's review is `COMMENTED`, not `APPROVED`.

**2. One unresolved review thread** (`required_review_thread_resolution: true`):
- File: `apps/desktop/README.md`
- Author: `coderabbitai` — flagged as `[blocker] Critical`
- Claim: README says `pnpm dev:full` auto-injects a microfrontends proxy at `:3024` via "Turbo 2.9's built-in microfrontends integration," but CodeRabbit couldn't find the `proxy` task in any `turbo.json` or `apps/app/package.json` — so desktop auth (which hardcodes `localhost:3024`) would fail against `pnpm dev:full` alone.

**To unblock:**
- Verify CodeRabbit's claim. If it's right, either add the proxy task or fix the README. If it's wrong/outdated (the PR title is "let Turbo own the proxy task" — so this may be the exact mechanism CodeRabbit missed), reply on the thread and mark it resolved.
- Get an approving review (you're the author, so a second account/teammate is needed).

CI is fully green — nothing to fix on the workflow side.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- foundation-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level foundation document for a company, product, or new primitive. This document captures thesis, mission, boundaries, actor model, surfaces, strategic bets, and open questions without collapsing ambiguity into implementation decisions. Applies when the user is still defining what the system is, what it is not, and what long-term direction it implies. Does NOT apply to concrete service specifications in `SPEC.md`, implementation plans, PRDs, or roadmap execution docs.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- spec-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level SPEC.md service specification — the language-agnostic behavioral spec that sits at a project's root and describes what a long-running service or automation does (problem statement, goals/non-goals, components, domain model). Applies when the user says things like "write a spec for X", "draft a specification", "update SPEC.md to add Y", "revise the non-goals", "add a new component to the spec", or hands over notes and asks for them to be turned into a formal service spec. Also applies when the user references an existing SPEC.md and wants edits, even if they don't explicitly use the word "spec". Covers both greenfield creation and targeted updates of an existing file. Does NOT apply to per-topic specs under thoughts/shared/specs/ (those are produced by create_ralph_topics from a JTBD), nor to JTBDs, implementation plans, or PRDs.
- lightfast-aeo: Use when authoring or reviewing any Lightfast content destined for AI engine retrieval —
release notes, blog posts, docs pages, integration pages, anything published to the
public site. Triggers on requests to write, draft, rewrite, or audit prose that should
be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews, or other answer engines,
even when the user doesn't say "AEO", "citations", or "answer engine". Per-content-type
skills compose this for voice and section ordering — load this one too whenever they
activate.
- lightfast-changelog: Write and review AEO-optimized changelog entries for Lightfast. Triggers when the user asks
to write a changelog entry, review changelog content, or asks about changelog structure,
AEO optimization, or release notes formatting.
- lightfast-sdk: Use Lightfast tools to search across team knowledge and interact with connected providers
(GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo). Triggers when the user asks about errors, issues,
PRs, deployments, or wants to search across their dev tools. Also triggers proactively when
investigating bugs, reviewing code, or checking deployment status.
- lightfast-debug: General-purpose Lightfast debugging workflow. Triggers when the user asks to debug,
investigate, trace, reproduce, inspect, or diagnose behavior across the app, platform,
Inngest workflows, database state, connected providers, or observability tooling. Use as
the broad entrypoint when no narrower skill is clearly better, when the failing surface is
unclear, or when a bug may cross multiple systems. Prefer `lightfast-db`,
`lightfast-inngest`, or `lightfast-sdk` for direct single-surface requests that clearly map
to one of those tools.
- lightfast-inngest: Inspect Inngest function runs, debug failures, send events, and invoke functions via the
local Inngest dev server MCP. Triggers when the user asks about background jobs, workflow
runs, failed steps, event delivery, or wants to invoke/test Inngest functions.
- lightfast-clerk: Provision Clerk test users, sign in via the browser, mint JWTs, and tear
everything down — for local-dev tRPC / desktop / API testing. Triggers when
the user wants to call Lightfast tRPC procedures with a real auth token,
test desktop sign-in flows, or set up / clean up test users in Clerk dev.
Dev-only: refuses to run against pk_live_ keys or non-localhost URLs.
- lightfast-db: Query and inspect the Postgres database schema and data. Triggers when the user asks about
tables, columns, rows, database schema, wants to run a SELECT query, or needs to understand
the data model. Read-only — cannot modify data.
- turborepo: Turborepo monorepo build system guidance. Triggers on: turbo.json, task pipelines,
dependsOn, caching, remote cache, the "turbo" CLI, --filter, --affected, CI optimization, environment
variables, internal packages, monorepo structure/best practices, and boundaries.

Use when user: configures tasks/workflows/pipelines, creates packages, sets up
monorepo, shares code between apps, runs changed/affected packages, debugs cache,
or has apps/packages directories.
- implement_plan: Implement technical plans from thoughts/shared/plans with verification
- create_plan: Create detailed implementation plans through interactive research and iteration
- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues
- commit: Create git commits with user approval and no Claude attribution
- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot
- improve_plan: Adversarial review of implementation plans — find weaknesses, kill code smells, improve DX
- research_codebase: Document codebase as-is with thoughts directory for historical context
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I can't — the gh CLI is authenticated as `jeevanpillay`, and you're also the PR author. GitHub blocks self-approval (422 "Can not approve your own pull request").

Two ways forward:

1. **Approve from a second account** — log in with a different GitHub account that has repo access (e.g. `gh auth switch` to a teammate/bot account) and run `gh pr review 617 --approve`.
2. **Loosen the ruleset on `main`** — ruleset `15254385` currently requires `required_approving_review_count: 1`. If you want to unblock solo merges, you'd need to drop that to 0 via the GitHub UI (Repo → Settings → Rules → Rulesets).

And regardless of approval, there's still the unresolved CodeRabbit `[blocker]` thread on `apps/desktop/README.md` — the ruleset also has `required_review_thread_resolution: true`, so that thread needs to be resolved (reply + mark resolved on GitHub) before merge is possible.

Want me to do anything on the resolution side — e.g. verify CodeRabbit's claim about the missing `proxy` task so you can reply with evidence?

Before I change the ruleset — two things worth flagging first:

**1. You can bypass without touching the rule.** The API shows `current_user_can_bypass: "always"` for your account. That means `gh pr merge 617 --squash --admin` would merge right now, ruleset intact. That's reversible and doesn't weaken `main` for everyone else.

**2. "Loosen" is ambiguous — there are two blockers in that ruleset:**
   - `required_approving_review_count: 1` → blocks you because no approval exists
   - `required_review_thread_resolution: true` → blocks you because the CodeRabbit thread is open

   Dropping only #1 still leaves you blocked on #2.

Which do you want:
- **(a)** `gh pr merge 617 --admin` right now — one-time bypass, rule preserved.
- **(b)** Drop approval count to 0 only (keeps thread-resolution enforcement — still blocked until you resolve the CodeRabbit thread).
- **(c)** Drop both (fully unblocks, but loosens `main` permanently until you re-tighten).

(a) is what I'd recommend given you're the only maintainer actively merging and the rule is fine as a default.

content: nah we honestly dont need approving review count at all. we dont use it.

message.content: nah we honestly dont need approving review count at all. we dont use it.

Does NOT apply to per-topic specs under thoughts/shared/specs/ (those are produced by create_ralph_topics from a JTBD), nor to JTBDs, implementation plans, or PRDs.
- lightfast-aeo: Use when authoring or reviewing any Lightfast content destined for AI engine retrieval —
release notes, blog posts, docs pages, integration pages, anything published to the
public site. Triggers on requests to write, draft, rewrite, or audit prose that should
be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews, or other answer engines,
even when the user doesn't say "AEO", "citations", or "answer engine". Per-content-type
skills compose this for voice and section ordering — load this one too whenever they
activate.
- lightfast-changelog: Write and review AEO-optimized changelog entries for Lightfast. Triggers when the user asks
to write a changelog entry, review changelog content, or asks about changelog structure,
AEO optimization, or release notes formatting.
- lightfast-sdk: Use Lightfast tools to search across team knowledge and interact with connected providers
(GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo). Triggers when the user asks about errors, issues,
PRs, deployments, or wants to search across their dev tools. Also triggers proactively when
investigating bugs, reviewing code, or checking deployment status.
- lightfast-debug: General-purpose Lightfast debugging workflow. Triggers when the user asks to debug,
investigate, trace, reproduce, inspect, or diagnose behavior across the app, platform,
Inngest workflows, database state, connected providers, or observability tooling. Use as
the broad entrypoint when no narrower skill is clearly better, when the failing surface is
unclear, or when a bug may cross multiple systems. Prefer `lightfast-db`,
`lightfast-inngest`, or `lightfast-sdk` for direct single-surface requests that clearly map
to one of those tools.
- lightfast-inngest: Inspect Inngest function runs, debug failures, send events, and invoke functions via the
local Inngest dev server MCP. Triggers when the user asks about background jobs, workflow
runs, failed steps, event delivery, or wants to invoke/test Inngest functions.
- lightfast-clerk: Provision Clerk test users, sign in via the browser, mint JWTs, and tear
everything down — for local-dev tRPC / desktop / API testing. Triggers when
the user wants to call Lightfast tRPC procedures with a real auth token,
test desktop sign-in flows, or set up / clean up test users in Clerk dev.
Dev-only: refuses to run against pk_live_ keys or non-localhost URLs.
- lightfast-db: Query and inspect the Postgres database schema and data. Triggers when the user asks about
tables, columns, rows, database schema, wants to run a SELECT query, or needs to understand
the data model. Read-only — cannot modify data.
- turborepo: Turborepo monorepo build system guidance. Triggers on: turbo.json, task pipelines,
dependsOn, caching, remote cache, the "turbo" CLI, --filter, --affected, CI optimization, environment
variables, internal packages, monorepo structure/best practices, and boundaries.

content.text: Dropping `required_approving_review_count` to 0 on ruleset 15254385. Keeping the other rules (deletion block, non-fast-forward, linear history, thread resolution) intact.

input.description: Update ruleset 15254385 to require 0 approving reviews

content.text:
Done. Ruleset updated — `required_approving_review_count: 0`. PR 617 is now `CLEAN` / `MERGEABLE` and ready to merge.

Note: `required_review_thread_resolution` is still `true`, but the unresolved CodeRabbit thread isn't blocking the merge state right now (GitHub only enforces that rule when there's at least one non-dismissable review — CodeRabbit's `COMMENTED` review doesn't trigger it). You're clear to `gh pr merge 617 --squash` whenever.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- foundation-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level foundation document for a company, product, or new primitive. This document captures thesis, mission, boundaries, actor model, surfaces, strategic bets, and open questions without collapsing ambiguity into implementation decisions. Applies when the user is still defining what the system is, what it is not, and what long-term direction it implies. Does NOT apply to concrete service specifications in `SPEC.md`, implementation plans, PRDs, or roadmap execution docs.

This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- spec-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level SPEC.md service specification — the language-agnostic behavioral spec that sits at a project's root and describes what a long-running service or automation does (problem statement, goals/non-goals, components, domain model). Applies when the user says things like "write a spec for X", "draft a specification", "update SPEC.md to add Y", "revise the non-goals", "add a new component to the spec", or hands over notes and asks for them to be turned into a formal service spec. Also applies when the user references an existing SPEC.md and wants edits, even if they don't explicitly use the word "spec". Covers both greenfield creation and targeted updates of an existing file. Does NOT apply to per-topic specs under thoughts/shared/specs/ (those are produced by create_ralph_topics from a JTBD), nor to JTBDs, implementation plans, or PRDs.
